coredata.dc:title: Facile synthesis of highly graphitized nitrogen-doped carbon dots and carbon sheets with solid-state white-light emission
coredata.dc:creator: Zheng, X.G.
Wang, H.L.
Ding, G.Q.
Cui, G.L.
Chen, L.
Zhang, P.H
Gong, Q.
Wang, S.M.
coredata.dc:description: We report the hydrothermal synthesis of nitrogen-doped carbon dots (NCDs) and carbon sheets (NCSs) with glucose and ammonia as precursors. The formation and N-doping of NCSs were one-step obtained. NCSs are highly graphitized with six-fold symmetry single-crystal character. The optical properties of NCDs and their application as single-phase solid-state phosphors toward white light-emitting diodes (LEDs) were thoroughly investigated. NCDs solutions exhibit strong blue-green emission under ultraviolet (UV) light illumination and possess excitation-dependent photoluminescence (PL) behavior. Irradiated by UV LEDs, NCDs solutions show broad PL in visible spectral region. To resist self-quenching of NCDs powder resulting from aggregations, poly (vinyl alcohol) (PVA) was added into NCDs solution. Without adding any other phosphors, solid-state NCDs/PVA composite demonstrates direct white-light emission. Our research offers a valuable reference for growing highly graphitized NCSs by facile hydrothermal method and developing white UV-LEDs with environment-friendly and metal-free NCDs phosphors.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Carbon materials
Hydrothermal method
Fluorescence
Nitrogen doping
White light-emitting diodes (LEDs)

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: TEM image (a) and HRTEM images of NCDs (b) and NCSs (c). FTIR spectrum (d) and XPS survey scan (e) of NCDs and NCSs. The inset of (e) is high-resolution XPS survey scan of N1s.

(a) UV–Vis absorption and PL excitation spectra of NCDs. Inset are photographs of NCDs solutions under white (left) or UV (right) light. (b) PL spectra of NCDs excited by various excitation wavelengths. In the inset, the emission spectral intensities are normalized.

(a) Emission spectra of different UV LEDs with or without NCDs coating; (b) Emission spectra of UV LED (∼395nm) with and without NCDs/PVA composite coating. Left inset: photographs of the coated UV LED without (top) and with applied voltage (bottom). Right inset: the CIE chromaticity coordinates for the illuminating UV LED with NCDs/PVA layer.

head.ce:title: Facile synthesis of highly graphitized nitrogen-doped carbon dots and carbon sheets with solid-state white-light emission

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Institute of Condensed Matter Physics, Linyi University, Linyi 276005, China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Physics, Qufu Normal University, Qufu 273165, China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: c
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Shanghai Institute of Microsystem and Information Technology, Shanghai 200050, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Shanghai Institute of Microsystem and Information Technology

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: We report the hydrothermal synthesis of nitrogen-doped carbon dots (NCDs) and carbon sheets (NCSs) with glucose and ammonia as precursors. The formation and N-doping of NCSs were one-step obtained. NCSs are highly graphitized with six-fold symmetry single-crystal character. The optical properties of NCDs and their application as single-phase solid-state phosphors toward white light-emitting diodes (LEDs) were thoroughly investigated. NCDs solutions exhibit strong blue-green emission under ultraviolet (UV) light illumination and possess excitation-dependent photoluminescence (PL) behavior. Irradiated by UV LEDs, NCDs solutions show broad PL in visible spectral region. To resist self-quenching of NCDs powder resulting from aggregations, poly (vinyl alcohol) (PVA) was added into NCDs solution. Without adding any other phosphors, solid-state NCDs/PVA composite demonstrates direct white-light emission. Our research offers a valuable reference for growing highly graphitized NCSs by facile hydrothermal method and developing white UV-LEDs with environment-friendly and metal-free NCDs phosphors.

ce:section.ce:para: Common phosphor-converted white light-emitting diodes (LEDs) involve ultraviolet (UV) LEDs combined with red-greenblue (RGB) phosphors or blue LEDs combined with yellow phosphors. Considering that the degradation of blue chips may cause serious color changes, it would be promising to develop white LEDs by UV pumping [1]. RGB phosphors usually contain rare-earth metals, which have potential toxicity and environmental hazardousness. Additionally, the mining and refining of phosphor materials are gradually restricted due to severe pollution, which provides opportunities to develop new low-toxicity fluorescent materials. As environment-friendly materials, fluorescent carbon dots have attracted considerable attention owing to their superiority in high quantum yield, low cost, environmental friendliness and excellent photostability [2]. Since carbon dots have these advantages, they may be a good light converter for LEDs. There have been some reports on white LEDs using carbon dots and semiconductor quantum dots as phosphors [3,4], but these semiconductor quantum dots involve toxic Cu2+ or Cd2+ etc. Once pure carbon dots are successfully used as single-phase white phosphors, their low-cost and low-toxicity properties would offer promising applications.

To this date, graphene quantum dots or graphene sheets were rarely synthesized by hydrothermal canonizing small molecules because hydrothermal carbon from molecular precursors is very difficult to well control the 2-dimention growth. N-doping graphene has great potential for electronics and spintronics applications [5]. N-doping can tune the electrical properties for high-frequency graphene devices and enhanced catalysis applications by introduce additional n-type carriers [6,7]. N-doping also has a significant impact on magnetoresistance in graphene [8,9]. Recently, researchers demonstrated the incorporation of N substituents in graphene using hyperthermal ion implantation (HyTII) [5,10]. This doping method is easily transferable to other dopant species and other two-dimensional atomic crystals [9,10]. N-doped graphene can also be synthesized by introducing ammonia gas during chemical vapor deposition (CVD) growth of graphene [6–8,11]. Usually, these methods need ultrahigh vacuum techniques, high growth temperature, complicated and expensive fabrication process. It is useful to develop easy and less expensive N-doping methods. In this letter, we reported the synthesis of highly graphitized N-doped carbon dots (NCDs) and carbon sheets (NCSs) via hydrothermal method. We also used solid-state NCDs as direct white light-emitting phosphors.

Glucose solution was prepared with the concentration of 5wt%. 35mL glucose solution and 0.05mL ammonia were put into a Teflon-lined autoclave. The reactor was heated to 180°C at a rate of 6°C/min and kept 180°C for 48h. After reaction, the reactor was cooled down to room temperature naturally. The solutions were further purified via dialysis for several days in distilled water. To demonstrate solid-state white-light emission of NCDs, some PVA was dissolved in NCDs solution and then coated an UV LED with the colloidal solution. After the water was evaporated, the LED was found to be coated with a brown layer. All chemicals were purchased from Alfa Aesar.

Infrared spectroscopy was performed using a Nicolet 6700 Fourier transform infrared (FTIR) spectrometer. X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) was performed using an Escalab 250 spectrometer with an Al X-ray source. Transmission electron microscopy (TEM) was performed on JEOL, JEM-2100F at operating voltage of 200kV. Emission spectra of the NCDs solution and the LED were recorded using a Cary eclipse fluorescence spectrophotometer with Xe lamp as an excitation source.

As shown in Fig.1 a, the as-prepared sample consists of two types of carbon nanomaterials. One is monodispersed NCDs with sizes of 6.2±1nm, and the other is NCSs between individual NCDs. High-resolution TEM (HRTEM) images (Fig.1b and c) show that NCDs and NCSs possess well-resolved lattice fringes with lattice spacings of 0.34 and 0.24nm, which are in close match with the (002) and (100) diffraction facets of graphite, respectively [12]. The highly graphitized NCSs have six-fold symmetry single-crystal character (inset in Fig.1c). The hydrothermal carbon dots with well-graphitized cores have been reported, but highly graphitized graphene quantum dots or graphene sheets were usually obtained from crystallized graphite by electrochemical method. There are few reports about hydrothermal canonizing carbohydrate molecules to graphitized carbon sheets due to the difficulty of controlling the 2D growth and restricting the 3D particle growth in hydrothermal process of hydrothermal carbon from molecular precursors. The long reaction time and the addition of ammonia may be responsible for the hydrothermal synthesis of large NCDs and highly graphitized NCSs. The exact formation mechanism still requires further study, but inspired by these findings, we could envision that the facile and low-cost synthesis of well-graphitized NCSs will make this material promising for the related applications of carbon-based microelectronic technologies. FT-IR analyses (Fig.1c) were used to identify the surface chemistry of the as-prepared samples. An obvious CC stretching at 1637cm−1 is a characteristic band of sp2 carbon in graphite [13]. The characteristic stretch band of the amine III C–N bond is at 1458cm−1 [14]. In order to further confirm the elemental composition and functional groups on NCDs and NCSs, a series of XPS analysis were carried out, as shown in Fig.1d. Three peaks at 285.0, 399.0 and 532.5eV are attributed to C1s, N1s and O1s, respectively. Nitrogen and carbon atomic percentages are 7.7 and 60.17at.%, suggesting that these NCDs and NCSs are N-doped and carbon-rich. The inset of Fig.1d shows the high-resolution XPS spectra of N1s. The N1s spectrum shows two peaks at 399.2 and 400.4eV, which are attributed to C-N-C and N-(C)3 groups, respectively [15].

Though the obtained samples consist of NCDS and NCSs, the below optical properties are mainly attributed to NCDs due to nearly zero bandgap of NCSs [16]. Fig.2 a depicts the UV–Vis and PL excitation (PLE) spectra of NCDs. There is an absorption peak at 270nm assigned to the π–π∗ transition of aromatic sp2 domains. In the PLE spectrum of NCDs, two peaks at 250 and 396nm are related to two kinds of electron transitions, indicating that there are at least two types of excitation energy trapped on the surface of NCDs [17]. Fig.3 b shows the PL spectra of NCDs excited by a series of wavelengths. The broad emission spectra are located in the visible region with peaks red-shifted from 420 to 530nm as the excitation wavelength ranging from 325 to 475nm. Different energy levels formed by different functional groups are responsible for the excitation-dependent PL property of NCDs. The PL quantum yield of NCDs was determined to be 10.2%, which is comparable with previous report [14].

Under ultraviolet light illumination, NCDs solutions exhibit strong blue-green emission (inset of Fig.2a), suggesting that NCDs may be used as white light-emitting phosphors. We coated a few drops of NCDs solution onto three commercially UV LEDs (centered at 365, 395, 405nm, respectively). As shown in Fig.3a, the intensity of UV lights was reduced, accompanied with the presence of a broad visible emission. PL peak shifts to red when the UV LED changes from 365 to 395 and 405nm. After drying, we found the emission of NCDs layer degraded seriously due to fluorescence self-quenching in the aggregated states. To solve this issue, we dissolved some PVA in NCDs solution to prevent the aggregation. In this case, PVA acts as flexible media like water. After the water was evaporated, the solid-state NCDs/PVA composite shows free-standing, flexible and exhibits strong PL under UV illumination, indicating the aggregation of NCDs did not occur in solid matrix. With suitable thickness of NCDs layer, the spectrum of the UV LED (∼395nm) consists of two parts including very weak UV light from UV chip and a strong visible light from solid-state NCDs/PVA composite (Fig.3b). The coated UV LED UV emitted white light (left inset of Fig.3b). UV light is invisible for naked eyes, which means NCDs layer exhibit direct white emission. The Commission International d'Eclairage (CIE) chromaticity coordinates for the NCDs coating were calculated to be (0.28, 0.37) from the emission curve.

In summary, we have demonstrated a facile way to synthesize highly graphitized NCDs and NCSs. In comparison to the previous reports, three features become apparent: 1) the formation and N-doping of NCSs were one-step obtained by hydrothermal method, which makes the growth of NCSs low-cost and facilitates the subsequent processing of carbon-based microelectronic products; (2) we demonstrate direct white emission using NCDs as single-phase phosphors; (3) we develop a facile approach to resist self-quenching of NCDs in solid state. This study provides a valuable reference for growing well-graphitized NCSs and developing white UV-LEDs with environment-friendly and metal-free NCDs phosphors.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: We thank financial support from the National Natural Science Foundation of China (Grant Nos. 11404158, 61321492, 11274151 and 51431004), Shandong Province Natural Science Foundation (Grant Nos. ZR2014FM011, BS2015CL003 and BS2014CL014), Doctoral Scientific Research Foundation of Linyi University (Grant No. LYDX2016BS070).
